content:
If you look in include/linux/alloc_tag.h you will find that many allocation functions are wrapped in alloc_hooks() using a #define. This macro is declared as follows:

// a few different variants of this one depending on config
#define DEFINE_ALLOC_TAG(_alloc_tag)                        \
    static struct alloc_tag _alloc_tag __used __aligned(8)          \
    __section(ALLOC_TAG_SECTION_NAME) = {                   \
        .ct = CODE_TAG_INIT,                        \
        .counters = NULL };

#define alloc_hooks_tag(_tag, _do_alloc)                \
({                                  \
    typeof(_do_alloc) _res;                     \
    if (mem_alloc_profiling_enabled()) {                \
        struct alloc_tag * __maybe_unused _old;         \
        _old = alloc_tag_save(_tag);                \
        _res = _do_alloc;                   \
        alloc_tag_restore(_tag, _old);              \
    } else                              \
        _res = _do_alloc;                   \
    _res;                               \
})

#define alloc_hooks(_do_alloc)                      \
({                                  \
    DEFINE_ALLOC_TAG(_alloc_tag);                   \
    alloc_hooks_tag(&_alloc_tag, _do_alloc);            \
})

So this means that each call to kmalloc expands to declaring a new global variable for counting allocations from this call-site.

Right now, all Rust code is going to share a single global declared in rust/helpers/, so alloc_hooks() combines all Rust code throughout the entire kernel. The INLINE_HELPERS patch doesn't help here (ok actually it helps a bit, because .ko modules get a separately compiled copy of helpers, so each module has its own global). To really fix it, we would need some way for things such as KBox::new to expand to a new instance of this static.

But we can't really create these statics using Rust statics, afaict. We want them duplicated per callsite, including when generics is in play.

What's the solution here? Is the only way to convert the C static into an inline asm block containing a section block? I think those do get duplicated by inlining and generics ...

Thoughts?

I made this about alloc_hooks(), but I don't think it's the only case in the kernel where this pattern occurs.
We could solve it similarly to how we solve lock class keys, with a macro.
But KBox::new isn't a macro today

Right. We could add a new kbox constructor that takes an alloc_tag, similar to lock class, and add a macro that generates the tag when calling the new constructor. We can then convert users to the new API over time.
A lot of churn, but I don't know how to do it without a macro.
Or rather, if we can do it without a macro, we can do the lock class thing without a macro as well.
I do think that inline asm works, but it might be a bit much ...
since it takes something that's not platform specific and makes it platform specific
How would you do it with inline asm?
Inline asm can be used to define global variables
With pushsection
It would still live inside KBox::new, there is nothing the inline asm block can read that would help it decide where we are called from. Unless you read the link register?
Assuming that the call to kmalloc gets inlined, then the inline asm would be inlined with it. Duplicating the inline asm through inlining leads to multiple copies.
In that case, would it work to define a static variable in pure rust in KBox::new and then force inlining of the function?
That static variable would not be duplicated.
it would be equivalent to declaring the static outside of the function at the top-level
Ok. But one you define in assembly would be?
yeah I believe so
As far as I can tell from pushsection docs, it allows you to switch the ELF section momentarily (with a stack). What section would you switch to that is unique for the invocation site?
Can you do something like some_name_{PC} or something like that?
It looks like the C code uses a #define called ALLOC_TAG_SECTION_NAME for the section name.
They do not have to be unique.
Multiple instances of the same section name are concatenated.
Each alloc call site would map to a unique counter, but they all live in the same section, which is ALLOC_TAG_SECTION_NAME?
So how would you ID what counter to use?
alloc_hooks_tag takes a pointer to the entry in the section
Right, so for my mental model, this pointer is equivalent to our lock class key. How do you obtain that pointer at the call site?
You could write the inline asm so that it stores the address in a specific register.
Then it would be an address somewhere inside the big section, to where this particular piece ended up.
OK, I guess that could work. Then we just have to be sure that the code is inlined.
And, as an added bonus we can use the same method to get rid of all the macros we have for lock class key. But the cost would be that constructors must be inlined.
I don't the use of target specific inline assembly should deter us. But I guess that is up for discussion.
Adding this for potential discussion at BoF
